bibliographic.citation: John Bruce, ed., Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV. in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI. A.D. M.CCCC.LXXI. (London: Printed for the Camden Society by John Bowyer Nichols and Son, 1838).
bibliographic.publisher: Camden Society; printed by John Bowyer Nichols and Son
bibliographic.title: Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV. in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI. A.D. M.CCCC.LXXI.

entities.description: First complete publication of the long English chronicle, with title page, introduction, transcription on numbered pp. 1-40, notes on pp. 41-47, and index. CME00129 selects the edition title page and chronicle only.
entities.label: John Bruce's 1838 Camden Society edition of the Historie of the Arrivall
notes: Bruce explicitly states that the published work is a copy of Stow's manuscript.
The edition retains 26 visible square-bracketed supplies in the CME-selected chronicle and prints three one-word blanks; notes following p. 40 discuss manuscript readings and emendations.

rights.basis: The edition was first published in 1838 and Bruce died in 1869. The UK life-plus-70 literary term and 25-year published-edition typographical term have expired; a work first published in 1838 is also outside current United States federal copyright terms.
rights.component: John Bruce's 1838 edition text, introduction, notes, and typographical arrangement; modern scans excluded

next_steps: Search Fleetwood and Stow inventories, correspondence, and manuscript descriptions for the identified source book.
Do not call the exemplar an authorial autograph or fifteenth-century manuscript without direct evidence.
openQuestions.question: What were the date, physical character, earlier provenance, and textual status of the lost Fleetwood exemplar copied by Stow?

next_steps: Inspect full-color images of ff. 31r-49r and the unfoliated boundary leaves, especially title, hands, quiring, and physical evidence bearing on whether the target is a separate or inserted unit.
Ask BL staff to reconcile Bruce's another-hand/D'Ewes account with the current folio-specific custodial statement.
notes: The aggregate statement that Stow wrote the manuscript is not silently expanded to the title page Bruce assigns to another hand.
openQuestions.question: Who supplied the title page associated with Harley MS 543 ff. 31r-49r, and was the apparently separate target unit physically inserted into the codex; if so, how and when did that occur given that the current D'Ewes catalog range excludes ff. 31-49?

next_steps: Consult modern textual studies and current editions of all surviving versions.
Model no short French or English version as a direct ancestor of CME00129 until the disputed transmission direction is resolved.
openQuestions.question: What is the direction and sequence among the long English Arrivall, shorter English text, and French newsletter versions?

next_steps: Ask BL Reference Services whether preservation photography, institutional viewers, or unlinked IIIF assets exist.
If no complete surrogate exists, request a whole-manuscript color quote with the exact surface inventory recorded in access metadata.
openQuestions.question: Does any public or institutionally restricted complete digital facsimile of Harley MS 543 exist outside the current BL catalog links?

next_steps: Ask U-M whether a different physical copy or transcription source was used.
If the source is the inspected printing, correct the XML in a separate encoding change while retaining the prior reading in lineage history.

next_steps: Seek repository history, import scripts, or maintainer confirmation before strengthening version_of to copied_from.

next_steps: Ask Internet Archive and, if necessary, PIMS-University of Toronto before redistributing the scan files themselves.

relations.subjectLabel: Andrew R. Whittle, The Historical Reputation of Edward IV 1461-1725 (2017)

reviewNotes: The primary subject is the repository artifact at CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00129.xml. The anonymous medieval work, lost Fleetwood exemplar, Stow transcript, Bruce edition, U-M encoding, IA facsimile, provider metadata, and BL catalog are distinct layers.

The primary transmission path is repository artifact -> U-M encoding -> Bruce 1838 edition -> Stow transcript in Harley MS 543 ff. 31r-49r -> lost Fleetwood exemplar. Bruce must not be connected directly to the Fleetwood exemplar.

The chronicle's author is anonymous. 'Servant of Edward IV' and eyewitness/participant-report access are internal claims supported by the work and discussed by modern scholarship, not independently confirmed identity facts.

Bruce's title page and complete chronicle are discontiguous in the scanned volume: physical PDF p. 5/BookReader n5 and physical PDF pp. 21-60/BookReader n21-n60. Introduction pp. iii-xiv, a blank page, notes pp. 41-47, and the index are not encoded.

Bruce states that the copy in Stow's hand entered the Harleian Library through Sir Simonds D'Ewes, but the current aggregate BL catalog says D'Ewes's A.450.ix covered ff. 1-30 and 50-181, conspicuously excluding the target ff. 31-49. The locus-level custodial history remains unresolved.

The current BL record describes the whole codex as paper, 210 x 150 mm, with ff. 181 plus twelve unfoliated leaves. A whole-manuscript imaging request should include all folios, rectos, versos, blanks, flyleaves, interposed unfoliated leaves, binding surfaces, and the target ff. 31r-49r mapping.

Whittle's 2017 thesis is supporting scholarship only and is not placed on the primary transmission path. Its cached 359-page PDF renders and yields text, but qpdf reports nonfatal linearization hint-table mismatches.

Bruce's claim that a Ghent French text is an abridgment is not promoted into the direct chain because modern scholarship described by Whittle disputes the direction and sequence of the long and short versions.

The XML contains 26 literal square-bracketed supplies and three blank GAP elements matching Bruce's printed blanks. These are observed edition and encoding features, not a complete reconstruction of Stow's manuscript conventions.
The XML title page reads PUBLISHED FOR where the inspected 1838 title page reads PRINTED FOR. The discrepancy is preserved as an open question rather than silently normalized.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository representation of University of Michigan CME00129. The U-M item encodes John Bruce's 1838 Camden Society edition title page and the complete chronicle on numbered pp. 1-40, while omitting Bruce's introduction, notes, index, and other volume matter. Bruce explicitly says his published text copies John Stow's late-sixteenth-century transcript, now British Library Harley MS 543, ff. 31r-49r. Stow's transcript in turn identifies its source as a book formerly belonging to William Fleetwood; that exemplar is now reported lost, and it is not established as an authorial autograph. The anonymous chronicle internally describes its compiler as a servant of Edward IV who witnessed much of the 1471 campaign and learned the remainder from participants. A complete 88-page Internet Archive PDF, complete OCR, IA metadata JSON, exact BL catalog JSON, and Andrew Whittle's complete 2017 thesis are cached with fixity. No public complete digital facsimile of Harley MS 543 was found; the exact target locus is ff. 31r-49r, and current BL routes permit physical consultation and paid imaging requests subject to availability and condition.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous (described as a servant of Edward IV)
catalogSummary.region: England (Yorkist royal milieu; exact place unknown)
catalogSummary.title: Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI

catalogingSubject.label: Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous, pro-Yorkist prose chronicle of Edward IV's return and restoration in 1471. It comprises three internally introduced narrative parts and claims compilation by a servant of Edward IV from personal observation and participant reports. It is not the later title page, Harley MS 543 as a whole, the lost Fleetwood exemplar as a physical object, John Bruce's complete 1838 Camden Society volume, the University of Michigan encoding, the Internet Archive scan, or the checked-in XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Fauconberg's assault on London and final settlement

locators.value: within ff. 31r-49r; exact internal folio boundary not publicly verified

contentParts.summary: Thomas Neville, the Bastard of Fauconberg, attacks London; the assault fails, Edward returns, and Fauconberg surrenders his ships at Sandwich on 26 May.

assertion.notes: The narrative reaches 26 May 1471 and presents itself as a contemporary servant's account. The exact completion date and place are unstated.

dateStatements.display: 1471, after 26 May; probably composed soon after the narrated campaign

assertion.notes: The surviving Stow transcript is dated by the current BL catalog to the fourth quarter of the sixteenth century. The 1471 composition and lost Fleetwood exemplar are distinct from this first surviving attestation; reports of earlier circulation do not supply an extant earlier copy.
Whittle identifies Bruce's Camden Society edition as the first publication of the complete long English text; earlier derivative use in Holinshed is not treated as publication of this version.

extent.description: One prose chronicle in three internally introduced narrative parts, occupying Bruce's numbered pp. 1-40; the separate encoded edition title page is editorial paratext and is not counted as a work part.

formStatements.description: Continuous prose chronicle divided into three internally introduced narrative parts.

assertion.notes: The surviving long version is late Middle English. Modern English title-page and editorial matter in Bruce's edition are representation layers, not additional work-level languages.

notes: William Fleetwood is the recorded former owner of the lost exemplar and the source of a downstream adapted narrative in Holinshed, not the author of the Arrivall under the inspected evidence.
The Fleetwood exemplar is not labeled an authorial autograph; its exact date and physical history are unresolved.
The three GAP elements encode blanks visible in Bruce's print and are not evidence of lacunae caused by present damage to Harley MS 543.

next_steps: Review the specialist studies cited by Whittle and later scholarship.
Retain anonymous authorship and treat institutional or shared-authorship proposals as hypotheses until direct evidence is established.
openQuestions.question: Can the anonymous servant of Edward IV be connected securely to the privy seal or to the author of the Lincolnshire Chronicle?

next_steps: Consult modern textual studies and editions of all versions.
Do not place a French short text upstream of this work solely on Bruce's nineteenth-century abridgment claim.
openQuestions.question: What is the direction and sequence among the long English Arrivall, shorter English text, and French newsletter versions?

next_steps: Check whether U-M encoded a different physical copy or whether this is a transcription error.
Correct the XML only in a separately scoped encoding change after verification.
openQuestions.question: Why does CME00129 transcribe the 1838 title page as PUBLISHED FOR when the inspected page reads PRINTED FOR?

assertion.notes: The account is written from Edward IV's English royal/Yorkist perspective. No inspected source fixes its place of composition more precisely.
The social role and eyewitness basis are asserted by the text. The author remains unnamed, and Whittle treats more specific institutional or shared-authorship proposals as hypotheses.
Stow transcribed the lost exemplar formerly in William Fleetwood's possession; his late-sixteenth-century copy survives in Harley MS 543, ff. 31r-49r.

scope.description: The 1838 Camden Society transcription, title, punctuation, supplied readings, and notes

subjects.label: Wars of the Roses and political news

assertion.notes: The narrative's partisan framing is explicit; propaganda is a modern analytical classification supported by Whittle.

summaries.value: A strongly pro-Yorkist account of Edward IV's departure from Zeeland, return to England, recovery of support, victories at Barnet and Tewkesbury, defeat of Thomas Neville's assault on London, and final settlement in May 1471. Its opening claims compilation by an unnamed royal servant who witnessed much of the campaign and obtained the remainder from participants.

CME00129 encodes Bruce's edition title page and the complete chronicle on numbered pp. 1-40. It omits Bruce's introduction, the blank leaf before the chronicle, notes on pp. 41-47, the index, and all other volume matter. Within the chronicle it preserves all three narrative parts, their arguments, and the final Fleetwood-book trailer.

The XML contains two DIV1, three DIV2, two HEAD, three ARGUMENT, 51 P, 41 PB, three GAP, three HI, 21 body SUP, and one TRAILER elements. Twenty-six literal square-bracketed supplies occur in the encoded chronicle. The three GAP elements represent one-word blanks printed by Bruce, not demonstrated physical damage. The encoded title page says PUBLISHED FOR where the inspected 1838 page says PRINTED FOR.

assertion.notes: An established access title, normalized only by omitting the date suffix and final punctuation; the surviving title page in Harley MS 543 is cataloged as later or in another hand rather than authorial.

titles.value: Historie of the Arrivall of Edward IV in England and the Finall Recouerye of His Kingdomes from Henry VI
The Historie of the Arrivall of King Edward IV

assertion.notes: Current BL catalog transcription of the title attached to the Harley witness; Bruce says its title page is in another hand.

titles.value: The Historie of the arrivall of K. Edward IV. in England and the finall recoverie of his kingdomes from Henry VI. in Anno Domini 1471
In the yere of grace 1471, aftar the comptinge of the churche of England

work.author: Anonymous (described as a servant of Edward IV)

work.summary: A strongly pro-Yorkist account of Edward IV's departure from Zeeland, return to England, recovery of support, victories at Barnet and Tewkesbury, defeat of Thomas Neville's assault on London, and final settlement in May 1471. Its opening claims compilation by an unnamed royal servant who witnessed much of the campaign and obtained the remainder from participants.
